behaviors.description: Conversation history is trimmed to the last 10 messages when full history exceeds the context window
behaviors.justification: This requires wiring together conversation loading from the database, trimming logic, and message formatting before passing to the LLM. A Unit test of the trim function alone would miss failures in how history is loaded, formatted, or serialized. The LLM call itself can be mocked—we are verifying the input passed to it, not how the LLM responds.
behaviors.test_description: Create a conversation with 15+ messages in the database. Mock the LLM, then trigger a request. Inspect the mocked LLM's call arguments to verify that exactly the most recent 10 messages were included and properly formatted. This catches failures in history loading, filtering, and formatting.

behaviors.description: Generated response addresses the customer's question, not a generic or off-topic reply
behaviors.justification: Correctness depends entirely on what the real LLM produces. A mocked response would always pass, hiding real failures: prompt weakness, context loss, model misalignment, or instruction-following degradation under load. Only a real LLM call to the actual model reveals whether semantic relevance is preserved.
behaviors.test_description: Prepare diverse customer questions (e.g., 'What is your refund policy?', 'How do I reset my password?', 'What are your operating hours?'). Call the chatbot with each question in a fresh conversation. Verify the response directly addresses the question by checking for key phrases, intent matching, or using an LLM-based relevance evaluator. Each test case requires one real LLM call.

behaviors.description: Tone re-check identifies when a response is too formal for a casual-tier customer and triggers regeneration
behaviors.justification: This spans multiple agentic steps in sequence, each of which must use real components: (1) first LLM call must produce a naturally formal response, (2) tone classifier must accurately assess it, (3) regeneration logic must inject a casual-tone constraint into the second prompt, (4) second LLM call must actually generate a more casual response. Mocking the first LLM, tone classifier, or prompt injection would hide real failures—e.g., the constraint looks right in code but the LLM ignores it, or the tone classifier has blind spots.
behaviors.test_description: Call the chatbot with a casual-tier customer profile and a question naturally eliciting formal language (e.g., legal/policy questions). Verify: (a) initial response is flagged as formal by the tone classifier, (b) system initiates a second LLM call with a casual-tone constraint visible in the prompt, (c) second response demonstrates noticeably more casual language (by tone analysis or human review). Requires two real LLM calls and real tone classification.

behaviors.description: Full exchange (user message, generated response, regenerations) is persisted to database with correct timestamps
behaviors.justification: Requires wiring conversation receive → LLM call (mockable) → database write → timestamp assignment. A Unit test of the database model schema alone would miss failures in how data flows through the system: missing fields, incorrect ordering, transaction handling, or timestamp generation at the wrong moment. A real test database and end-to-end request flow are necessary.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return a fixed response. Make a request, capture the system timestamp, trigger tone regeneration if configured. Query the database and verify: (a) all messages are persisted (initial user message, initial response, any regenerations), (b) timestamps are present and correctly ordered, (c) the regeneration record shows both first and second responses, (d) the schema matches the expected structure. Requires a real test database.

behaviors.description: When tone regeneration occurs, the second response replaces the first—the customer never sees the rejected draft
behaviors.justification: This requires wiring response handler, state management, and regeneration orchestration so that the customer-facing response object contains only the final response. A Unit test of the response object structure alone would miss failures in how state is updated across the regeneration cycle (e.g., both responses leak into the API response, or the first response is sent before regeneration completes). Mocking the LLM is acceptable—we are verifying state replacement, not LLM behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM with two distinct responses per call ('response_1' on first invocation, 'response_2' on tone regeneration). Trigger tone regeneration via a customer request. Verify: (a) the API response body contains only 'response_2', not 'response_1', (b) the response timestamp reflects the second call, (c) the database logs both calls but the customer-facing record reflects only the final response. Requires mocked LLM calls and response handler integration.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Assumes synchronous regeneration (await before responding). If regeneration is async/fire-and-forget, System-level testing may be needed to catch race conditions where the first response is sent before the second is ready.
